perturbation.original_text: Inktomi shall strictly adhere to all Security Measures in performing the Services, including without limitation securing the Usage Data, which it may possess or have under its control from time to time, from unauthorized access and modification.
perturbation.changed_text: Inktomi shall endeavor to adhere to the described Security Measures in performing the Services, including taking steps to secure the Usage Data, which it may possess or have under its control from time to time, from unauthorized access and modification.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall strictly adhere' to 'shall endeavor to adhere' weakens the obligation. Replacing 'securing' with 'taking steps to secure' further diminishes the definiteness of the action required. This creates ambiguity regarding the required level of security and allows for subjective interpretation of compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CCPA requires businesses to implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information to protect personal information. The modification introduces ambiguity about Inktomi's obligation to protect user data, potentially falling short of the CCPA's requirement for 'reasonable security,' thus creating a contradiction. The term 'endeavor' could be interpreted as less than reasonable security. Microsoft and its customers may face penalties if Inktomi does not maintain sufficient levels of data security and a data breach happens.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens Inktomi's obligation to secure usage data, potentially contradicting the CCPA's requirement for 'reasonable security procedures and practices.' The original text states Inktomi 'shall strictly adhere' to security measures, while the modified text states they 'shall endeavor to adhere,' which introduces ambiguity. The original contract requires 'securing' the data, but the modified version only requires 'taking steps to secure' it. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that consumers can institute a civil action if their personal information is subject to unauthorized access as a result of the business’ violation of the duty to implement and maintain reasonable security procedures. By weakening the language around security, the changed text increases the risk of violating this duty.

perturbation.original_text: Microsoft agrees to comply with the Security Measures at all times when accessing the Hosting Servers as permitted hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Microsoft agrees to consider the Security Measures when accessing the Hosting Servers as permitted hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to comply' to 'agrees to consider' weakens Microsoft's obligation to follow security measures. This creates uncertainty about the level of adherence required and introduces subjectivity.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Data Breach Notification Law requires businesses to implement and maintain reasonable security procedures. By only agreeing to 'consider' security measures, Microsoft's obligation becomes vague and may not meet the requirement for reasonable security, potentially contradicting the law. If Microsoft's failure to comply with security measures causes a breach, leading to a data leak, this would violate Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.29.

(a) Any agency that owns or licenses computerized data that includes personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach in the security of the data to any resident of California (1) whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person, or, (2) whose encrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the encryption key or security credential was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the agency that owns or licenses the encrypted information has a reasonable belief that the encryption key or security credential could
perturbation.justification: The original text states Microsoft 'agrees to comply' with security measures, which imposes a direct obligation. The changed text weakens this to 'agrees to consider', introducing ambiguity and potentially allowing Microsoft to disregard the measures. This creates an ambiguity as defined by the Perturbation Type, making the obligation difficult to enforce. Snippet 1 from Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.29(a) mandates agencies that handle computerized data to disclose any security breach. By weakening the obligation to comply with security measures, the changed text increases the risk of a breach, potentially contradicting the requirement for reasonable security procedures implied by the law.

perturbation.original_text: Inktomi shall keep true and accurate books and records, in accordance with Generally Accepted Accounting Principles ("GAAP"), relating to all costs and expenses for which Inktomi is entitled to charge Microsoft pursuant to Section 4.1 above, throughout the Term and for eighteen (18) additional months thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: Inktomi shall keep records, which it believes to be accurate, relating to all costs and expenses for which Inktomi is entitled to charge Microsoft pursuant to Section 4.1 above, throughout the Term and for eighteen (18) additional months thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'true and accurate books and records, in accordance with Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP)' with 'records, which it believes to be accurate' introduces ambiguity. It removes the explicit requirement to adhere to GAAP and replaces it with a subjective belief of accuracy, potentially leading to discrepancies and disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act mandates that public companies maintain accurate financial records and internal controls. By removing the GAAP requirement and allowing Inktomi to keep records they 'believe to be accurate', the contract weakens the standard of financial record-keeping, potentially conflicting with the SOX requirement for accurate and reliable financial reporting. Although Inktomi is not explicitly a public company in the contract, the services it is providing to Microsoft, a public company, could be indirectly impacted.

Corporate responsibility for financial reports (a) Regulations required The Commission shall, by rule, require, for each company filing periodic reports under section 78m(a) or 78o(d) of this title , that the principal executive officer or officers and the principal financial officer or officers, or persons performing similar functions, certify in each annual or quarterly r
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'true and accurate books and records, in accordance with Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP)', which sets a clear, objective standard for financial record-keeping. The changed text replaces this with a subjective standard: 'records, which it believes to be accurate.' This introduces ambiguity and weakens the requirement for adherence to GAAP. The Law Explanation states that this change 'weakens the standard of financial record-keeping, potentially conflicting with the SOX requirement for accurate and reliable financial reporting.' While the Scraped Law Snippet 1 speaks to 'Corporate responsibility for financial reports' under 15 USC 7241, this is undermined by the weakening of the GAAP requirement in the contract, making it ambiguous and difficult to enforce accurate reporting standards. This falls under Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation. The removal of a concrete requirement weakens the legal obligation, making compliance uncertain and potentially leading to disputes.
